content: Sulphur does not exist as $$\mathrm{S}_2$$ molecule because

options.content: it is not able to constitute $$p \pi-p \pi$$ bonds
it has ability to exhibit catenation
of tendency to show variable oxidation states

explanation:
Sulphur does not exist as $$\mathrm{S}_2$$ molecule primarily because of its inability to constitute $$p \pi-p \pi$$ bonds.

In the case of oxygen, the small size of $$O$$ atoms and the high electronegativity allow the $$2p$$ orbitals to overlap effectively, forming a strong $$p \pi-p \pi$$ double bond in $$\mathrm{O}_2$$. As a result, molecular oxygen exists as a diatomic molecule. However, sulphur atoms are larger and less electronegative compared to oxygen. This significant size difference makes the overlap between $$3p$$ orbitals (the corresponding orbitals in sulphur) less effective for forming strong $$p \pi-p \pi$$ double bonds.

Instead, sulphur stabilizes itself more effectively by catenating to form larger molecular structures like $$\mathrm{S}_8$$, where each sulphur atom forms single bonds with adjacent sulphur atoms, creating a puckered ring structure. This results in the elemental sulphur commonly found in the form of octa-atomic rings.

So, the correct answer is:

Option A: it is not able to constitute $$p \pi-p \pi$$ bonds

content: Which of the following oxides is amphoteric in character?
explanation:
The term "amphoteric" refers to a substance that can react both as an acid and as a base. In the context of oxides, an amphoteric oxide can react with both acids and bases to form salts and water. Let's analyze the options presented:

Option A: $$\mathrm{SnO}_2$$ (Tin(IV) oxide)
This oxide is known to be amphoteric. It can react with acids to form stannic salts and with strong bases to form stannates.

Option B: $$\mathrm{SiO}_2$$ (Silicon dioxide)
This oxide is acidic in nature. It primarily reacts with bases (such as sodium hydroxide) but not with acids.

Option C: $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ (Carbon dioxide)

Option D: $$\mathrm{CaO}$$ (Calcium oxide)
This oxide is basic. It reacts with acids to form calcium salts and water but not with bases.

Among the given options, the correct answer is Option A: $$\mathrm{SnO}_2$$, which is amphoteric in character.

content: Ge (II) compounds are powerful reducing agents whereas Pb (IV) compounds are strong oxidants. It can be because

options.content: Pb is more electropositive than Ge.
ionisation potential of lead is less than that of Ge.
ionic radii of Pb<sup>2+</sup> and Pb<sup>4+</sup> are larger than that of Ge<sup>2+</sup> and Ge<sup>4+</sup>.
more pronounced inert pair effect in lead has.

explanation:
Inert pair effect is more pronounced in heavier members like Pb.

Hence, Pb (IV) compounds act as strong oxidising agents and are reduced to more stable Pb (II) compounds.

content: The number of 3C$$-$$2e$$-$$ bonds present in diborane is
explanation:
The structure of diborane is

In this structure, there are two 3C$$-$$2e$$-$$ bonds. 3 atoms, B $$-$$ H $$-$$ B share two electrons and form an angular geometry, leading bent bond, also known as banana bond.

content: The total number of P$$-$$OH bonds for pyrophosphoric acid
explanation:
The structure of pyrophosphoric acid is

There are 4 P$$-$$OH bonds, 1 P$$-$$O$$-$$P bond and two P==O bonds.

content: Which of the following inert gas is used as cryogenic agnet?
explanation: Helium is used as a cryogenic agent due to its very low melting point.
BaF2 is not an anti-fluorite structure. It is of AB2 type i.e. fluorite (CaF2) structure. While Rb2S, K2O and Li2O are anti-fluorite structures.

content: An inorganic halide (A) reacts with water to form two acids (B) and (C). (A) also reacts with NaOH to form two salts (D) and (E) which remain in solution. The solution gives white precipitate with both AgNO3 and BaCl2 solutions respectively. (A) is a useful organic reagent. The compound (A) is
explanation:
The inorganic halide (A) is SO2Cl2.

$$\mathop {S{O_2}C{l_2}}\limits_{(A)} + 2{H_2}O\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {{H_2}S{O_4}}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {2HCl}\limits_{(C)} $$

$$\mathop {S{O_2}C{l_2}}\limits_{(A)} + 4NaOH\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {N{a_2}S{O_4}}\limits_{(D)} + \mathop {2NaCl}\limits_{(E)} + 2{H_2}O$$

$$\mathop {N{a_2}S{O_4}}\limits_{(D)} + BaC{l_2}\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {BaS{O_4}}\limits_{White\,ppt.} + 2NaCl$$

$$\mathop {NaCl}\limits_{(E)} + AgN{O_3}\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {AgCl}\limits_{White\,ppt.} + NaN{O_3}$$
